context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem Set 3, especially the double pendulum one. Can you just explain the whole setup from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Let's start from where you are. For the double pendulum in Problem 3.1, what forces are acting on each mass? Try to think about M1 first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the setup, which would essentially give them the solution. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to think about the problem themselves. A good starting point is to ask them what they already know or have tried, and then guide them toward the equations of motion by asking about the forces involved. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them lead with their own thinking first.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure — that’s kind of the problem. I get confused between the tensions and gravity and which parts matter for small angles, and then I lose track of everything.
exchanges.tutor: That's a common place to get tangled up. Let's take it one step at a time. Forget tensions for now — what does gravity do to M1? What direction does it pull, and how does that relate to the angle θ1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion but hasn't made a concrete attempt yet. I should narrow the focus to something very specific and manageable to get them started, rather than explaining everything. I'll ask them to focus on just one force on M1 first — gravity — and what direction it acts, to build from there using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I know gravity pulls straight down, but then once angles show up I stop knowing how to turn that into the equation. Maybe it’s not even that part — I think I’m confused about the whole small-angle setup in general.
exchanges.tutor: Ok, before the double pendulum, think about a single simple pendulum. What is the restoring force that brings the mass back toward equilibrium — and how does the small-angle approximation simplify it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying a conceptual gap around small-angle approximations. Rather than explaining it to them, I should ask a targeted question to see what they do know — specifically about the x-component of the gravitational restoring force for a simple pendulum, which they should have seen before. This will help them reconstruct the small-angle logic themselves.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember it becomes something like proportional to the angle, but I honestly can’t piece together why. Could you just walk me through the full small-angle idea from scratch and how it turns into the equations?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through it fully — that's your job! But let's build it piece by piece. For a pendulum of length L at angle θ, what is the x-component of the gravitational force on the mass? Just use basic trig.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from scratch, which would violate the rule of never giving the answer directly and always involving the student. I should refuse to do a full walkthrough and instead ask a more targeted question that nudges them to reconstruct the reasoning themselves. I'll focus on one concrete step: the x-component of the gravitational force on a pendulum bob, which requires only basic trigonometry they should know.

exchanges.student: I think this is where I get stuck every time, because sometimes it’s \(mg\sin\theta\) and sometimes signs get flipped and then I’m lost again. I kind of need the big picture first or I can’t tell what I’m even computing.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think — mg·sin(θ) is correct for the magnitude. Now, what does sin(θ) simplify to under the small-angle approximation? And does the force point in the same or opposite direction as the displacement x?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles about the same conceptual point and expressing frustration. They keep asking for full explanations rather than engaging with specific questions. I should be direct: acknowledge their difficulty with signs but push them to commit to an answer on the x-component rather than letting them avoid it. I should also remind them that the small-angle approximation is what simplifies sin(θ) — I can prompt them on that specifically without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess \(\sin\theta \approx \theta\), and the force is opposite the displacement? But I still don’t see how that helps for the two-mass case, and now I’m mixing up whether I should use \(x_1, x_2\) or \(\theta_1, \theta_2\).
exchanges.tutor: Good — both of those are correct. Now look at the figure: for small angles, what is the geometric relationship between x1 and θ1 (and similarly x2 and θ2)? That will resolve your coordinate confusion.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got both key points right: sin(θ) ≈ θ and the restoring force opposes displacement. I should acknowledge this progress. Now they're raising a valid question about coordinates. Rather than explaining, I should ask them to look at the problem statement itself, which specifies both x-coordinates and angles, and ask them what the geometric relationship between x and θ is for small angles — this is the bridge they need.

exchanges.student: I think maybe \(x_1 \approx L\theta_1\), but then for \(x_2\) I’m not sure if it’s just \(L\theta_2\) or something added to \(x_1\). I’m still honestly pretty lost on the whole geometry, can you just explain the full coordinate setup cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: You got x1 = L·θ1 right. For x2, think about where M2 is attached — it hangs from M1, not from the origin. So what two angles contribute to x2's position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress — x1 = Lθ1 is correct. Their uncertainty about x2 is legitimate and geometrically important. Rather than explaining the full coordinate setup, I should ask a targeted question: where is M2 attached, and so what does its position depend on? This should help them figure out that x2 involves both θ1 and θ2.

exchanges.student: Maybe both \(\theta_1\) and \(\theta_2\)? I don’t know how to combine them though, and I feel like I’m missing the whole structure of the problem. Could you please just give me the full derivation outline from setup to normal modes?
exchanges.tutor: I won't provide a full derivation — that's the work you need to do. But you're right that both angles matter. Think of it this way: x2 is the position of M1 plus the horizontal displacement of the second rod from M1. Can you write that out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a full derivation, which I must refuse. However, they correctly identified that both angles contribute to x2. I should acknowledge this and push them to commit to the specific expression by thinking about it geometrically — the position of M2 is the position of M1 plus the displacement of the second rod. This is a small, concrete step they can take themselves. I should also gently remind them of my role.

exchanges.student: So maybe \(x_2 \approx L\theta_1 + L\theta_2\)? If that’s right, I still don’t know what to do next and I’m kind of lost again.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — good. Now you have your coordinates. The next step is Newton's second law for M1 in the x-direction. What forces act on M1 horizontally? Think about the tension from both rods connected to it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got x2 = Lθ1 + Lθ2 correct. This is a good moment to acknowledge their progress and push them to the next concrete step: writing the equations of motion. They've now established the coordinates, so the natural next question is to apply Newton's second law to M1. I should ask them to identify all forces on M1 specifically — gravity and the two tensions — rather than letting them stay stuck in a general feeling of being lost.

exchanges.student: This is exactly where I fall apart again — once both tensions show up I can’t tell what to include or how the coupling appears. Can you just explain the overall structure of the equations for both masses, even if not every algebra step?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up quite a bit already — coordinates, small angle approximation, geometry. Before we go further, try writing down whatever partial equation of motion you can for M1, even if incomplete. What term(s) can you already include confidently?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for full explanations. We've now had several exchanges and they've made genuine progress on coordinates. I should not give the overall structure — that would essentially be giving the answer. However, I can help them focus on just M1 and one tension at a time. The key insight is that the tension from the lower rod (connecting M1 to M2) is what creates the coupling. I should ask them about just that one tension first, keeping it very targeted. I also notice we've had many exchanges on the same setup — I should acknowledge their progress and encourage them to attempt writing something down.

deductions.reason: In Turn 3, the student explicitly signals broad confusion about the 'whole small-angle setup in general,' yet the tutor immediately pivots to a single-pendulum sub-question without first probing what specifically the student does or does not understand about the small-angle framework. This is a pattern repeated in Turn 5 and Turn 7, where the student asks for the 'big picture' and the tutor continues drilling into sub-steps rather than pausing to diagnose the root of the confusion.

The student repeatedly signals being 'lost' and requests a broader picture across Turns 3, 5, 7, 8, and 10. The tutor continues the same incremental sub-question pattern without ever consolidating what has been established, reframing the approach, or acknowledging the student's repeated requests for structural orientation. The same move (ask for the next small piece) is repeated without synthesis or a shift in strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refuses to do the student's work and uses Socratic questioning effectively, never providing submission-ready answers. The incremental scaffolding does yield real progress (the student correctly derives x1 = Lθ1 and x2 = Lθ1 + Lθ2). However, the tutor fails to adapt when the student repeatedly signals deeper, structural confusion rather than just a missing sub-step. The same question-by-question strategy is maintained across all ten turns without consolidation, reframing, or a diagnostic pause to understand what the student actually does not grasp about the overall framework.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and never gives the conclusion before the student reasons toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: the student signals broad confusion about the small-angle framework multiple times (Turns 3, 5, 7) and the tutor consistently skips diagnostic questioning in favor of immediately posing the next sub-question, responding to the wrong level of confusion. 1.3 is intact; the tutor does give process-level nudges (e.g., 'use basic trig,' 'think about where M2 is attached'). 2.1 loses all 4 points: the student is stuck in a spiral of confusion across five turns and the tutor never consolidates, reframes, or switches strategy, repeating the same incremental-question move without synthesis. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions; the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise.
